full_text:
Spatial patterns of immune cell states are associated with poor response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in high-grade serous ovarian cancer

Iga Niemiec1, Essi Kahelin1,2, Zhihan Liang1, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan3, Anna Vähärautio1, Anni Virtanen1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,4,5,6

INTRODUCTION

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

The composition and functional state of the tumor microenvironment (TME) play a crucial role in determining response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients. Understanding these complex interactions is essential for patient stratification and development of novel combination therapies. This study aimed to investigate the spatial distribution and functional states of different cell types within TME and their association with NACT response, in Visium spatial transcriptomics HGSC samples.

Our findings highlight substantial differences in TME composition depending on proximity to the tumor and NACT response. Macrophages, fibroblasts, T cells and plasma cells exhibited the most prominent spatial patterns. These results underscore the importance of studying the TME within a spatial context to gain deeper insights into these intricate interactions, so important to the NACT response.

Each spot on H&E slides have been manually annotated by a pathologist as tumor, mixed (both tumor and stroma morphology), stroma or other.

ions, so important to the NACT response.

Each spot on H&E slides have been manually annotated by a pathologist as tumor, mixed (both tumor and stroma morphology), stroma or other. Three zones of stroma in the tumor-stromal interface (TSI) were defined based on proximity to tumor/mixed spots.

8 HGSC post-NACT 10x Visum spatial transcriptomics samples from patients with different NACT response:
3 - good, 2 - partial, 3 - poor

Cell type distribution analysis within the zones was calculated using the FindTransferAnchor deconvolution method from the Seurat R package and reference scRNA HGSC dataset.

8 HGSC Visium samples with zones annotation

Spots annotations, deconvolution and UCell scores show good concordance

Mean z-score of deconvolution scores across the zones show differential, response-specific spatial distribution of cell types across the zones

Log2 fold change of deconvolution scores between good and bad responders

Distribution of deconvolution scores between zones and patients response

T-cells

Macrophages

Genes positively correlated with deconvolution scores (>0.7) have been identified across all groups and filtered to those unique for zone and response. Overrepresentation analysis (ORA) on Canonical Pathways from msigDB have been conducted to find pathways enhanced in every group.

MATERIALS & RESULTS

CONCLUSION

Additionally UCell scores for HGSC TME cell type signatures have been computed.
Mean scores have been compared across zones within each response group.
